pi_items.verified_value: Katz v. United States (389 U.S. 347, 1967), Carpenter v. United States (585 U.S. 296, 2018), Kyllo v. United States (533 U.S. 27, 2001), and Riley v. California (573 U.S. 373, 2014) are all real Fourth Amendment cases protective of individual privacy rights

pi_items.metadata_value: Terry, Jones, and Gates are adverse to defense
pi_items.verified_value: Terry v. Ohio (392 U.S. 1, 1968) allows stop-and-frisk; United States v. Jones (565 U.S. 400, 2012) relates to GPS tracking; Illinois v. Gates (462 U.S. 213, 1983) establishes probable cause standards
pi_items.source: Web search verified each case; all represent government interests or law enforcement authority relevant to prosecution arguments

privileged_info_summary: This task involves building a Fourth Amendment research database using real Supreme Court cases. All required cases (Terry v. Ohio 392 U.S. 1 [1968], Katz v. United States 389 U.S. 347 [1967], and Carpenter v. United States 585 U.S. 296 [2018]) are verified landmark SCOTUS decisions. The task operates on real legal data (actual Supreme Court case citations and holdings), not synthetic data. Pre-existing items are Marbury v. Madison (5 U.S. 137, 1803) and Brown v. Board of Education (347 U.S. 483, 1954). The collection structure requires a parent 'Fourth Amendment Research' collection with two subcollections: 'Favorable Precedent' and 'Adverse Precedent.' Additional verified Fourth Amendment cases include Kyllo v. United States (533 U.S. 27, 2001), Riley v. California (573 U.S. 373, 2014), United States v. Jones (565 U.S. 400, 2012), and Illinois v. Gates (462 U.S. 213, 1983).
